parsed.summary: The commit rebrands the npm package scope and adds an interactive re-configuration menu to the setup command. The npm scope rename is a breaking change for any existing consumer whose lockfile or scripts reference the old scope. The setup command changes (commands/setup.md) add interactive branching logic but, as a markdown prompt template, lack explicit guards for partially-configured or corrupted state detection. The release is ship-blocking on the scope rename alone unless a deprecation/migration path is documented.

findings.title: npm scope rename is a breaking change with no visible migration path
findings.body: package.json shows 4 lines changed, consistent with renaming the npm package scope (the commit message confirms 'rename npm scope'). Any existing user with a lockfile, CI pipeline, or script referencing the old scoped package name will break on upgrade. There is no evidence in the diff of a deprecation notice, a redirect/alias on the registry, or migration instructions in the changelog that would prevent silent 404s or wrong-version installs for existing consumers.
findings.recommendation: Publish the old scoped package as a stub that depends on the new one (or document the breaking change prominently in README and changelog). Verify the new scope is already published and resolves correctly before releasing.

findings.title: Setup menu added but no handling for partially-configured or corrupted state
findings.body: commands/setup.md has 19 lines changed adding an interactive menu 'when already configured.' As a markdown-based prompt template, any branching logic relies on the LLM correctly detecting configuration state. If state detection is incomplete (e.g., config file exists but is malformed, or required fields are missing), the menu may present misleading options or skip re-configuration, leaving the user in a broken state with no error signal.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit validation instructions in the setup.md template: if config exists, verify all required fields are present and well-formed before showing the 'already configured' menu. Include a 'force reconfigure' option that works regardless of existing state.

findings.title: release_card.md has large structural changes that may drift from actual plugin behavior
findings.body: release_card.md shows 326 lines changed (273 additions, 103 deletions), indicating a major rewrite. As the user-facing release documentation, any divergence between what it describes and what the plugin actually does (especially after the scope rename and setup menu changes) will create confusion. There is no automated validation that release_card.md stays in sync with actual plugin capabilities.
findings.recommendation: Manual review: verify all command names, npm scope references, and setup flow descriptions in release_card.md match the actual plugin after this commit. Consider a smoke test that greps for the old scope name across all docs.

next_steps: Verify the new npm scope is published and installable; confirm the old scope either redirects or has a clear deprecation stub.
Inspect commands/setup.md for explicit state-validation logic covering corrupt, partial, and missing config scenarios.
Search all changed documentation files (CHANGELOG.md, release_card.md) for any remaining references to the old scope name.
Add a changelog entry explicitly marking the scope rename as a breaking change with migration instructions.
